insights.insight: The protocol settles verified purchase confirmation at ~$0.10 per receipt versus $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic ad tech attribution — a 50–200x cost delta that isn't a negotiating point, it's a structural difference in what's being purchased: proof versus inference.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and fingerprinting under regulatory pressure, the ad industry's remaining cross-site signal layer is largely probabilistic inference from first-party data — the protocol's cryptographically verified, identity-free receipts are structurally different from anything currently available at scale.

insights.topic: Physical retail dominance in the dataset
insights.insight: Kroger, Walmart, Costco, Target, Publix, Dollar General, and Home Depot now dominate the verified receipt dataset — meaning the harder data problem (physical in-store purchases, not digital orders) is being solved, producing the category of purchase signal that has historically been least accessible to brand marketers.

Amazon sees Amazon purchases, Walmart sees Walmart purchases, banks see transaction amounts but not SKUs — this protocol's aggregate layer is the first system positioned to produce identity-free, cross-retailer basket data that no single platform can replicate from its own first-party data.

Active receipt data is now coming from rural and non-metro states including Kentucky, Maine, Wisconsin, and Tennessee — the dataset is spreading beyond urban early-adopter clusters, which matters because physical retail spend in non-metro markets represents a significant share of CPG and big-box volume that brand marketers consistently underindex.

insights.topic: Receipt scanning apps as a closed-loop comparison
insights.insight: Fetch and Ibotta hold real, verified purchase data but it is identity-attached, siloed, and not queryable by third parties — the structural difference here is identity-free data that can be queried across ecosystems without triggering GDPR/CCPA liability on a named consumer record.
